title: Is a dehumidifier cheaper than heating to dry the air?
description: A compressor dehumidifier costs about 5p an hour and actually removes water; heating just hides it. Full UK cost comparison at the current price cap.
blurb: Heating hides moisture, a dehumidifier removes it for about 5p an hour. We compare the real costs of each.
lede: Damp windows in the morning, a musty smell in one corner, washing that never quite dries. UK advice on this splits into two camps: one says turn the heating up and the moisture will go away, the other says plug in a dehumidifier. The two approaches cost very different amounts, and only one of them actually takes water out of your house. This guide puts real pence-per-hour figures on both, explains the physics in plain terms, and shows the situations where each one wins.
short_answer: Yes, in most cases a dehumidifier is far cheaper. A typical 200W compressor dehumidifier costs about 5.2p an hour at the current 26.11p per kWh electricity rate, and it physically removes water from the air. Running gas central heating costs roughly 50p an hour for a typical home and removes no water at all unless you ventilate the warm damp air outside. For condensation, damp and drying laundry indoors, the dehumidifier wins on cost and on results.

sections.h2: Why heating alone does not dry the air
sections.html:
Warm air can hold more water vapour than cold air. When you heat a room, the relative humidity falls and the air feels drier, but the actual amount of water in the room has not changed by a single gram. You have hidden the moisture, not removed it.

The problem comes back the moment things cool down. When the heating goes off in the evening, the air temperature drops, its capacity to hold water shrinks, and the moisture condenses out on whatever is coldest: window glass, the north-facing wall behind the wardrobe, the cold corner of a ceiling. That is exactly where mould takes hold.

There are only two ways to genuinely remove water from indoor air. You can ventilate it out, swapping moist indoor air for drier outdoor air, or you can condense it deliberately inside a machine and pour it down the sink. Heating supports both jobs, because warm surfaces stay above the dew point and warm air evaporates water faster, but on its own it is an expensive way to shuffle moisture around the house.

Modern airtight homes make this worse, not better. Good draught-proofing cuts heating bills, but it also removes the accidental ventilation that used to carry moisture away, so the water from cooking, showers and breathing has nowhere to go.

sections.h2: What each option costs per hour
sections.html:
Here is what the realistic options cost at current price cap rates, and crucially, whether they remove any water while running.

MethodTypical inputCost per hourCost for 8 hoursRemoves water?
Compressor dehumidifier200W electric5.2p42pYes
Desiccant dehumidifier600W electric15.7p£1.25Yes, and heats the room
Electric fan heater2,000W electric52.2p£4.18No
Gas central heating, typical home~7 kWh gas per hour51p£4.10No

At 26.11p per kWh electricity and 7.33p per kWh gas, Ofgem price cap July to September 2026. Heating figures assume the boiler is firing for the full hour in a typical three-bed home.

The gap is roughly ten to one. Eight hours of compressor dehumidifying costs about 42p and can pull several litres of water out of the air in a damp house. Eight extra hours of heating costs over £4 and pulls out none.

The other popular comparison is turning the thermostat up a degree and leaving it there. A 1°C increase adds roughly 10 per cent to your heating use, which works out around £84 a year for a home using 11,500 kWh of gas at 7.33p per kWh. A dehumidifier running 8 hours a day costs about £12.50 a month, and you only run it during the damp months. For full figures across different machine sizes and usage patterns, see our dehumidifier running cost guide.

sections.h2: The hidden bonus: a dehumidifier is also a small heater
sections.html:
People often assume the dehumidifier's running cost comes on top of their heating cost. In practice a good chunk of it comes back as warmth.

  • Every watt of electricity the machine uses ends up as heat in the room. A 200W compressor unit is, among other things, a 200W heater.
  • Condensing water releases energy. Every litre the machine collects gives back roughly 0.7 kWh of heat that was absorbed when that water originally evaporated. A unit collecting a few litres a day is quietly returning that energy to the room.
  • Drier air feels warmer. Most people are comfortable at a slightly lower thermostat setting when relative humidity sits around 50 per cent instead of 70, and damp walls and furnishings stop wicking heat away.

None of this turns a dehumidifier into a heating system. The output is similar to having an extra person or two in the room. But it does mean the effective cost of running one in winter is lower than the meter suggests, because almost nothing is wasted. A desiccant model takes this further: it draws more power, around 500 to 700W, but blows out noticeably warm air, so in a cold room it doubles as gentle background heating while it works.

sections.h2: Drying laundry indoors: the clearest win
sections.html:
This is where the dehumidifier argument is strongest. A typical washing load comes out of the spin cycle holding around 2 litres of water, and all of it has to go somewhere.

Drying on radiators or an airer with the heating on

Evaporating 2 litres of water absorbs roughly 1.4 kWh of heat from the room, heat your boiler paid to produce. The gas itself is cheap, around 10p worth, but the water does not vanish. It loads the air in the whole house and condenses on every cold surface overnight. Routinely drying washing this way is one of the most common causes of condensation and black mould in UK homes, and wet washing draped directly on radiators also blocks heat output and makes the boiler work harder.

Dehumidifier and airer in a closed room

Put the airer and the dehumidifier in a small room, shut the door, and run the machine for 6 to 8 hours. A 200W unit uses 1.2 to 1.6 kWh, costing 31p to 42p per load at 26.11p per kWh. The clothes dry properly, often faster than on a radiator, and the water ends up in the tank rather than on your windows. Many machines have a laundry mode that runs flat out then switches off via a humidistat.

How that compares with a tumble dryer

A condenser dryer uses around 4 kWh per load, about £1.04. A heat pump dryer uses around 1.5 kWh, about 39p, which is the same ballpark as the dehumidifier method but with a much higher purchase price. The full comparison, including outdoor drying and heated airers, is in our guide to the cheapest way to dry clothes.

sections.h2: When heating is the better answer
sections.html:
A dehumidifier is not the right tool for every damp problem, and there are real situations where warmth has to be part of the fix.

  • Very cold rooms. Compressor dehumidifiers lose capacity sharply below about 15°C and their coils can ice up below 10°C. In an unheated room in January a cheap compressor unit may collect very little. Either choose a desiccant model, which works fine in the cold and heats the room as it runs, or add some background heat so the compressor unit can do its job.
  • Mould on cold external walls. If a poorly insulated wall sits below the dew point, moisture will find it even at moderate indoor humidity. Lowering humidity with a dehumidifier helps, but the lasting fix is raising the surface temperature with insulation and consistent background heat.
  • Damp from a building fault. A leaking gutter, a failed damp proof course or penetrating rain will defeat any machine. A dehumidifier will mask the symptom while the wall quietly gets worse, so find and fix the source first.
  • When ventilation is free and effective. Cold winter air is dry once warmed up, so ten minutes of cross-ventilation after a shower or while cooking removes moisture for nothing. Extractor fans, trickle vents and lids on saucepans all reduce the amount of water a machine has to deal with. Our guide to cheap fixes for condensation and mould covers the no-cost habits worth trying before buying anything.

The best results in a chronically damp house usually come from a combination: modest steady heat to keep surfaces above the dew point, sensible ventilation at the moisture sources, and a dehumidifier to deal with what is left.

Per hour of running, a compressor dehumidifier costs around 5p against roughly 50p for gas central heating in a typical home, and only the dehumidifier removes any water. Trying to dry a damp house by turning the heating up is the most expensive option available and the moisture simply returns as condensation when the rooms cool.

Use each tool for its actual job. Heat the house for comfort and to keep surfaces above the dew point. Ventilate at the source when you cook, shower and dry washing. Run a dehumidifier to remove the moisture that remains, ideally on its humidistat so it only draws power when humidity climbs. A £5 hygrometer tells you where you stand: aim for 40 to 60 per cent relative humidity, and if your rooms sit above that all winter, a 42p-a-day dehumidifier will do more for damp than pounds a day of extra heating ever could.

faq.q: Does turning the heating up get rid of damp?
faq.a: No. Heating lowers relative humidity while the room is warm, which makes the air feel drier, but the water is still there. As soon as the room cools the moisture condenses back out on cold surfaces like windows and external walls. To remove water you have to ventilate it outside or collect it with a dehumidifier.

faq.q: How much does it cost to run a dehumidifier all day?
faq.a: A typical 200W compressor dehumidifier costs about £1.25 for a full 24 hours at 26.11p per kWh, the Ofgem cap rate for July to September 2026. In practice most people run one for around 8 hours a day, about 42p, or leave it on a humidistat so it only draws full power when humidity rises.

faq.q: Should I buy a compressor or desiccant dehumidifier for a cold house?
faq.a: Desiccant models work much better below about 15°C, where compressor units lose capacity and can ice up. A desiccant machine costs more per hour, roughly 15.7p at 600W versus 5.2p for a 200W compressor unit, but it keeps extracting in a cold room and blows out warm air, so it doubles as gentle background heating.

faq.q: Is a dehumidifier cheaper than a tumble dryer for drying clothes?
faq.a: Usually yes. A dehumidifier with an airer in a closed room uses about 1.2 to 1.6 kWh per load, around 31p to 42p, compared with roughly £1.04 for a condenser tumble dryer. A heat pump dryer costs about the same per load as the dehumidifier method but is much more expensive to buy.

faq.q: What humidity level should my home be?
faq.a: Aim for 40 to 60 per cent relative humidity, with about 55 per cent a good winter target. Below 40 per cent air feels uncomfortably dry; above 60 per cent condensation, dust mites and mould all become more likely. A basic digital hygrometer costs a few pounds and shows whether you actually have a humidity problem before you spend anything.
